interest: Equity advocates and equal-access constituencies (disadvantaged children, non-test-prepped families) want reforms that actually reduce sorting rather than displacing it, while excellence/choice constituencies want any cap to apply symmetrically across maintainers.
value: Equity of access is in tension with the illusion of equity reform — the claim exposes that a nominally equity-driven cap may deepen SES stratification rather than reduce it.
fear: The anticipated harm is that disadvantaged pupils face fewer accessible, transparent state seats while high-SES families migrate to less-accountable church/foundation schools, so segregation worsens under an equity banner.
affected: middle-SES families without coaching or relocation capacity
assumption: It assumes non-state maintainers have both the capacity and the incentive to absorb displaced selective demand and are not covered by the same caps or transparency rules, so demand relocates rather than disappears.
empirical_uncertainty: The residual-SES-skew effect is described by the scenario itself as mechanical and near-certain, while the magnitude of non-state absorption is flagged as documented-feasible but not quantified (weak confidence), so the direction is settled but the scale is contested.
